critic_raw:
This multi-spectral CNN versus RGB-only baseline experiment for algal biomass prediction is methodologically well-conceived: temporal split by year prevents data leakage, the preprocessing pipeline is disciplined, and the comparison correctly isolates the benefit of additional spectral bands by holding architecture constant between the CNN and baseline. The use of MAE as the primary metric is appropriate for continuous biomass estimation. However, several important issues warrant attention.

The experiment covers only summer months (June-August) from 8-12 lakes. This is an extremely narrow operational window that limits both generalizability and statistical power. The test set (year 5 summer, 3 months, 8-12 lakes) may contain only a few hundred image-label pairs—insufficient for confident evaluation of a CNN with millions of parameters. No sample size adequacy analysis is provided. The per-lake test MAE analysis is particularly vulnerable to small sample size: if some lakes have only 20-30 test observations, per-lake results are unreliable.

The experiment restricts labels to in-situ measurements within ±2 days of image acquisition. In practice, algal bloom dynamics change rapidly over timescales of days, and a ±2 day window introduces label-image temporal misalignment that acts as irreducible label noise. This noise floor is not quantified, which means the reported MAE cannot be properly contextualized: without knowing the expected label noise from temporal misalignment, it is impossible to assess whether the models are approaching the noise ceiling.

The multi-spectral CNN learns spectral relationships from raw bands without hand-crafted spectral indices (NDVI, NDCI, etc.). While the stated intent is to let the CNN discover these relationships, this approach requires sufficient training data to learn physically meaningful spectral combinations. With 3,000-5,000 image-label pairs across only 3 training years, the CNN may lack the data to reliably learn spectral index relationships, and the comparison may understate the multi-spectral advantage relative to what a model with 10x more training data would achieve.

The by-lake stratification described in the split keeps all lakes in both training and test. This means the model is evaluated on lakes it has seen during training (same lakes, different years), which measures interpolation within known water bodies rather than generalization to novel lakes. For operational deployment where the model would be applied to new lakes, this evaluation protocol is optimistic.
all_issues_raised: Summer-only data from 8-12 lakes yields a test set that may be too small (few hundred pairs) for reliable CNN evaluation; no sample size adequacy analysis provided
±2 day temporal misalignment between image acquisition and in-situ measurement introduces irreducible label noise that is not quantified, preventing proper MAE contextualization
CNN must learn spectral index relationships from scratch with limited training data; approach may understate multi-spectral advantage achievable with larger datasets
Evaluation tests generalization across years within known lakes, not generalization to novel lakes; protocol is optimistic relative to operational deployment scenario
Restricted to summer months only; no discussion of whether seasonal patterns would affect spectral-biomass relationships or whether results generalize to non-summer monitoring
Summer-only data restriction limits generalizability to bloom onset and decline — operationally important periods excluded from training and evaluation
±2-day image-to-sampling temporal offset introduces label noise correlated with bloom intensity, potentially biasing the comparison during high-bloom events
Per-lake test evaluation in year 5 is sensitive to anomalous bloom conditions in that year — no robustness checks for test-year lake-specific outliers
Single-patch spatial representation underrepresents spatially heterogeneous bloom dynamics — whole-lake predictions require spatial aggregation not described in the design
No evaluation of model performance during transitional bloom stages (onset, peak, decline) — temporal dynamics within the summer season are ignored
All lakes appear in both training and test sets; only temporal generalization is tested, not cross-site or new-lake generalization, limiting deployment claims
2-day temporal misalignment between satellite overpass and in-situ sampling introduces label noise from bloom dynamics; no sensitivity analysis for temporal lag effects
End-to-end spectral learning may overfit to instrument-specific band calibrations; no multi-sensor or sensor-change sensitivity analysis
RGB-only baseline is too weak; standard spectral indices (NDCI, chlorophyll-a band ratios) derived from additional bands represent a more appropriate intermediate baseline

No discussion of atmospheric correction quality as a confound: atmospheric correction errors in training vs. test years could confound the year-over-year spectral comparison independent of model quality
all_issues_adjudicated: Summer-only data from 8-12 lakes yields a test set that may be too small (few hundred pairs) for reliable CNN evaluation; no sample size adequacy analysis provided
